adme.excrete: Eliminated in urine as conjugated metabolites; characteristic metabolites (e.g. 6β-hydroxymetandienone and long-term 17-epimer metabolites) underpin WADA anti-doping detection. Elimination half-life ≈ 3–6 h.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. General androgen sensitivity tracks AR-gene CAG-repeat length, and CYP19A1 (aromatase) activity influences estrogen conversion and thus estrogenic side-effect load — but neither is characterised for metandienone specifically, so individual response should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: No approved therapeutic dose exists; it is a controlled substance. Historic clinical/research trials used 10–100 mg/day (Freed 10–25 mg/day; Hervey 100 mg/day). Gray-market physique use is commonly cited in a similar oral range, split through the day because of the short half-life — a description, not a recommendation, at doses never validated for safety.
tech.tolerance: There is no receptor-downregulation tolerance in the caffeine sense. The dominant time-dependent consequences are cumulative HPTA suppression (falling endogenous testosterone) and accruing hepatic and cardiovascular strain (cholestasis, hypertension, adverse lipid shifts) that build with continued use and recover variably after cessation — this, not tachyphylaxis, is what governs 'on/off' cycling in practice.

contrasts.title: Dianabol vs testosterone — the swallowable copy
contrasts.point: Same lock (the androgen receptor), same estrogen conversion — but one key difference in the chemistry changes everything. Testosterone is destroyed if swallowed, so it's injected; Dbol is testosterone with a 17α-methyl 'armour plate' added so it survives the liver and works as a pill. That convenience is the whole appeal — and the whole problem: the armour plate jams the liver's bile plumbing, making oral Dbol distinctly hepatotoxic in a way injected testosterone is not. Milder on the needle, harder on the liver.

contrasts.title: Dianabol vs ostarine — old-school flood vs filed-down key
contrasts.point: Both act on the androgen receptor, but from opposite design philosophies. Ostarine is a non-steroidal 'selective' modulator engineered to favour muscle over prostate and skin. Dbol is an unselective steroidal flood — it hits every androgen lock and aromatises to estrogen on top, which is why it carries the full package of gyno, hypertension, and liver strain rather than a trimmed side-effect profile. Both still shut down your own testosterone.

questions: If all three are "just steroids," why is one (Anadrol) infamous for water and puffy nipples while another (Masteron) is used to look dry and hard?
How can Anadrol give you estrogen-type side effects when, chemically, it can't turn into estrogen at all?
Why do people call Primobolan the "gentle, safe" steroid — and what exactly does "gentle" leave untouched?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) They share one engine — the androgen receptor — but differ in their side-channels: Anadrol drives water retention and estrogenic effects, while Masteron and Primobolan are "dry" DHT-derivatives that add no water, so the same muscle simply looks harder. (2) Anadrol is estrogenic even though it never aromatizes — it appears to switch the estrogen receptor on directly, so the water and gyno come without any estrogen being made. (3) "Gentle" describes Primobolan's low androgenic and low estrogenic side profile — it does NOT mean it spares your own hormones: like every androgen here, it still reads to your brain as testosterone and shuts your natural production down.

oxymetholone-anadrol-drostanolone-master.bigIdea: These three aren't three different drugs so much as one engine — the androgen receptor — sold in three tunings: Anadrol is the loud, powerful, dirty build (maximum mass, heavy liver load, paradoxical estrogen effects), while Masteron and Primobolan trade raw power for a drier, milder ride — and all three still switch off the testosterone your body makes.
oxymetholone-anadrol-drostanolone-master.analogy: Picture the same car engine sold in three trims. Anadrol is the drag-strip build: brutal power, runs hot, floods the engine (water retention) and wears the block (your liver). Masteron is tuned for a hard, dry finish — less power, cleaner lines. Primobolan is the economy tune: quiet, low-stress, modest output you can drive for a long time. Different trims, one engine — and every one of them trips the same alarm: your body sees the engine running and stops building its own.

mechSteps.t: All three are DHT-shaped keys for one receptor
mechSteps.predict: Testosterone can turn into estrogen; these three can't. What do they all have in common structurally that blocks that?
mechSteps.d: Oxymetholone, drostanolone and methenolone are all derivatives of DHT (dihydrotestosterone) — they're already in the 5α-reduced form, so none of them is a substrate for aromatase and none can convert into estrogen. Same target as testosterone, a DHT-based scaffold.

mechSteps.t: They dock the receptor and turn genes on
mechSteps.predict: The androgen receptor isn't a channel or a brake — it's a switch that does what, exactly?
mechSteps.d: Each binds the androgen receptor, which travels into the nucleus and binds DNA, turning up the genes for muscle protein synthesis (and, notably for oxymetholone, red-blood-cell production). This is transcription, so lean mass and strength build over weeks of dosing, not minutes.

mechSteps.t: The differences live in the side-channels, not the core
mechSteps.predict: If they hit the same receptor, why is one a water-bloated mass drug and another a dry cosmetic one?
mechSteps.d: Anadrol is 17α-alkylated so it survives being swallowed — the very feature that makes it hepatotoxic — and despite never aromatizing it activates the estrogen receptor directly, driving water retention and gynecomastia. Masteron and Primobolan are injectable (or non-17α oral) DHT-derivatives that add no estrogenic water, giving the dry, hard look. Same engine, different exhaust.

mechSteps.t: Your brain reads the signal and shuts you down
mechSteps.predict: Your body constantly measures its own androgen level. What happens when a drug floods that signal for weeks?
mechSteps.d: The hypothalamus and pituitary sense androgens present and dial down LH and FSH — the hormones that tell your testes to make testosterone. So even the "mild" ones suppress your own production. That HPTA shutdown is the headline cost, and "mild" side-effect profile does nothing to spare it.

framework.target: The androgen receptor (AR) — the same nuclear gene-switch testosterone uses. All three are full steroidal AR agonists (they turn it on throughout the body), not tissue-selective like a SARM. Because each is derived from DHT, they're already 5α-reduced and none can aromatize into estrogen.
framework.affinity: There's no single clean Ki to quote across the trio, and verifiable per-compound affinity numbers are thin — so treat any exact "Ki = …" claim about these with suspicion. The well-documented and instructive case is oxymetholone (Anadrol): it binds AR relatively weakly, yet it's one of the most potent mass-builders in the class. That mismatch is the lesson — its effect isn't AR affinity alone; it also stimulates erythropoiesis and, unusually, activates the estrogen receptor directly. Potency here is not a simple lock-and-key number.
framework.adme: The split that matters is oral vs injectable. Oxymetholone is an oral 17α-alkylated steroid — the alkyl group lets it survive first-pass liver metabolism, which is exactly why it's hepatotoxic. Methenolone comes as an oral acetate (a 1-methyl DHT, NOT 17α-alkylated, so much lighter on the liver) and an injectable enanthate; drostanolone is injectable-only as propionate or enanthate esters. But note: the felt effect is set by weeks of gene transcription, not by any blood-level curve — so ester half-lives govern injection frequency, not how fast muscle appears.
framework.genetics: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic switch exists for any of the three. AR sensitivity varies generally with the AR gene's CAG-repeat length, but this has not been characterised for these molecules — so individual response, and how hard your own axis is suppressed, should be treated as unpredictable.
framework.evidence: Uneven and honest. Oxymetholone has the real data: FDA-approved for anemias of deficient red-cell production and tested in randomised trials (HIV wasting). Drostanolone has a historical clinical past as a breast-cancer adjunct. Methenolone has almost no modern controlled human data. Critically, none of the three has been studied for long-term safety in healthy people using it to build a physique.

journey.d: Anadrol is swallowed — its 17α-methyl group lets it survive the liver's first pass, the same trick that makes it toxic. Masteron and Primobolan are usually injected as oil-based esters (Primobolan also as an oral acetate). Real caution: the black market is riddled with counterfeits, and Primobolan is among the most faked compounds in it, so bottle contents are often not what the label claims.
It circulates and seeks out androgen-receptor-bearing tissue. For the injectable esters, the oil depot releases the drug slowly over days to weeks, keeping blood levels steady rather than spiking.

It binds the androgen receptor, drives it into the nucleus, and turns up muscle-protein-synthesis genes — and, for oxymetholone especially, red-blood-cell production. Over weeks this is where lean mass slowly rises.
The hypothalamus and pituitary detect the androgen signal and throttle back LH and FSH — so your own testosterone winds down. This suppression builds over the cycle and outlasts the last dose.

With oral Anadrol this is where the damage shows: cholestatic jaundice, peliosis hepatis, and hepatic tumors are documented risks of 17α-alkylated steroids. As DHT-derivatives, all three also tend to sharply lower protective HDL cholesterol.
Metabolites leave in urine. All three are controlled anabolic steroids and WADA-banned in and out of competition, detectable long after the last dose — a long trail of positive doping tests.

myths.myth: Primobolan and Masteron are "mild," so they don't shut down your natural testosterone.
myths.truth: "Mild" describes their low androgenic and estrogenic side-effect profile — not mercy toward your own hormones. Every androgen here reads to your hypothalamus as testosterone and suppresses LH/FSH, so your natural production still shuts down.

myths.myth: Anadrol can't cause estrogen side effects because it doesn't aromatize.
myths.truth: It's markedly estrogenic anyway. Because it's DHT-derived it genuinely can't be converted to estrogen — but it appears to activate the estrogen receptor directly, producing the water retention and gynecomastia people wrongly assume are impossible.

myths.myth: Masteron and Primobolan "harden" muscle or burn fat.
myths.truth: They don't melt fat or physically harden tissue. The dry, hard look is the absence of estrogenic water retention combined with already-low body fat — the muscle simply looks sharper because there's no water blurring it, not because the drug is a fat-burner.

myths.myth: Injectable means it's safe for your liver.
myths.truth: Non-17α injectables are gentler on the liver than oral Anadrol — but "liver-friendly" is not "safe." The suppressed testosterone, crashed HDL cholesterol, and cardiovascular strain apply across the board, injectable or not.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use them — and what they cost:
items: **Why people reach for each:** Anadrol for the fastest scale-weight and strength jump (much of it water); Masteron for a dry, hard cosmetic finish, typically near a contest at low body fat; Primobolan for slow, keepable lean tissue with a low side-effect profile. These are the pitches — the costs below are why the pitch is only half the story.

**The liver cost (Anadrol especially):** as an oral 17α-alkylated steroid it carries documented risks of cholestatic jaundice, peliosis hepatis, and hepatic tumors. This was the standout adverse event even in monitored clinical trials.

**The hormonal and cardiovascular cost:** all three suppress your own testosterone (recovery after stopping is variable), and as DHT-derivatives they tend to sharply lower protective HDL cholesterol and can accelerate hair loss and prostate effects. Anadrol adds water retention and raised blood pressure.
**The legal and testing cost:** these are controlled anabolic steroids, WADA-banned in and out of competition and detectable long after use. For a tested athlete this ends seasons and careers.

**The product-quality cost:** these are gray/black-market drugs. Counterfeiting is rampant — Primobolan in particular is among the most faked — so what's in the vial is frequently underdosed, mislabeled, or a different compound entirely.

**The honest evidence picture:** oxymetholone has genuine clinical data (approved for anemia; tested in HIV wasting) but at medical doses under monitoring in sick patients — not healthy lifters. Drostanolone's human record is largely historical breast-cancer use, and methenolone has almost no modern controlled data. None has long-term safety data for physique use. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a cycle.

start.q: In one line: what do Anadrol, Masteron and Primobolan actually have in common, and how do they differ?
start.a: They share one mechanism — DHT-derived androgen-receptor agonists that build muscle by turning genes on. They differ only in side-channels: liver load, estrogenic behavior, and potency.

how.q: How can Anadrol cause estrogen-type side effects if it can't aromatize?
how.a: It doesn't make any estrogen — it appears to activate the estrogen receptor directly. So water retention and gynecomastia happen through the receptor, bypassing the aromatase step entirely.

use.q: Someone says Primobolan is "mild and safe." What does "mild" cover, and what does it not?
use.a: It covers a low androgenic/estrogenic side-effect profile. It does NOT cover HPTA suppression — Primobolan still shuts down your own testosterone like every androgen here.

evidence.q: Which of the three has real human trial data, and in whom?
evidence.a: Oxymetholone — it's FDA-approved for anemias and was tested in a randomised HIV-wasting trial, in sick patients at medical doses. The other two have thin-to-historical human data, and none is validated for healthy physique use.

canExplain: Why all three are DHT-derivatives that build muscle through the same androgen receptor, differing only in their side-channels
Why Anadrol produces estrogenic water retention and gynecomastia despite being unable to aromatize
Why 'mild' steroids like Primobolan still fully suppress your own testosterone
Why oxymetholone can be a top-tier mass builder while binding the androgen receptor only weakly
How strong the human evidence really is for each — real for oxymetholone in anemia and wasting, thin for the other two, and absent for long-term physique safety

selfTest.q: A friend argues Anadrol is 'safe from gyno' because it's a DHT-derivative that can't turn into estrogen. Where is his logic right, and where does it fail?
selfTest.a: He's right that it can't aromatize — being DHT-derived, it genuinely makes no estrogen. He's wrong on the conclusion: Anadrol is notably estrogenic anyway, apparently by activating the estrogen receptor directly, so water retention and gyno are real risks without any estrogen being produced.

selfTest.q: Two of these three are considered liver-friendly and one is notorious for liver damage. What single structural feature explains the split, and which compound has it?
selfTest.a: The 17α-alkyl group. Oxymetholone (Anadrol) is 17α-alkylated so it survives oral first-pass metabolism — the same feature that makes it hepatotoxic. Drostanolone (injectable) and methenolone (a 1-methyl DHT, not 17α-alkylated) lack that heavy liver load.

selfTest.q: Oxymetholone binds the androgen receptor only weakly, yet it's one of the strongest mass-builders here. Why doesn't low affinity mean weak effect?
selfTest.a: Because affinity isn't the whole mechanism. Oxymetholone still strongly drives AR-mediated transcription, and it adds effects the receptor number alone doesn't capture — potent stimulation of red-blood-cell production and direct estrogen-receptor activation (water/weight). Potency here comes from more than a single binding constant.

evi.howStrong: The evidence is real but uneven across the three, and almost none of it is about healthy people building a physique. Oxymetholone is the outlier with genuine data: it's FDA-approved for anemias of deficient red-cell production and was tested in randomised, placebo-controlled trials for HIV-associated wasting. Drostanolone's human record is largely historical — it was once used clinically as an adjunct in advanced breast cancer. Methenolone has very little modern controlled human evidence at all. So a 3/5 evidence rating reflects oxymetholone's clinical pedigree, not proof that these are safe or effective for bodybuilding.
evi.whatItShows: Where it's been tested, oxymetholone reliably adds body weight and lean/body-cell mass and raises red-cell counts in patients who are wasting or anemic. What the literature does NOT show is a favorable long-term safety profile in healthy users, or meaningful controlled physique data for drostanolone or methenolone.
evi.effect: In the phase III HIV-wasting trial, oxymetholone produced significant gains — on the order of ~3 kg of body weight and a body-cell-mass increase of roughly 3.8 kg in the twice-daily group — versus about 1 kg in placebo. The consistent shadow across the data is hepatotoxicity: liver-associated toxicity was the standout adverse event even under medical supervision.
evi.whoBenefits: The studied populations were sick: anemic patients and HIV patients losing muscle, where preserving lean mass and red cells is the medical goal, at monitored doses. Use by healthy people for muscle-building is entirely off-label, gray-market, and unstudied for safety.
evi.caveats: Read the trial numbers as best-case results in patients, under monitoring, with pharmaceutical-grade drug — not what a counterfeit-riddled black market delivers. Add near-universal testosterone suppression, sharply lowered HDL cholesterol, Anadrol's documented liver injury, the WADA ban, and the fact that these are controlled substances. Drostanolone and methenolone in particular rest far more on anecdote than on trials.

trials.finding: Randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase III trial in 89 HIV patients with wasting: oxymetholone significantly increased body weight (~3 kg) and body cell mass (~3.8 kg in the twice-daily group) versus placebo, with liver-associated toxicity the most important adverse event.
trials.ref: Hengge et al., 2003, AIDS

trials.finding: Comprehensive review of oxymetholone as a 17α-alkylated anabolic-androgenic steroid — approved for anemias of deficient red-cell production, studied in HIV wasting and other conditions, with hepatotoxicity (cholestatic jaundice, peliosis hepatis, hepatic tumors) as the major adverse effect.
trials.ref: Pavlatos et al., 2001, Clinical Therapeutics

tech.chem: All three are synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroids derived from dihydrotestosterone (DHT), so all are 5α-reduced and non-aromatizable. Oxymetholone (Anadrol; CAS 434-07-1) is 2-hydroxymethylene-17α-methyl-DHT — the 17α-methyl group confers oral activity and hepatotoxicity, and the 2-hydroxymethylene group is invoked to explain its paradoxical estrogenic activity. Drostanolone (Masteron) is 2α-methyl-DHT, the 2α-methyl group raising anabolic potency and resistance to metabolism; it is used clinically as the propionate or enanthate ester. Methenolone (Primobolan) is 1-methyl-5α-androst-1-en-17β-ol-3-one (a 1-methyl DHT); its 1-methyl/1-ene modification — not 17α-alkylation — gives the oral acetate its activity, which is why it is comparatively light on the liver.

targets.action: full steroidal agonist (non-selective across tissues)
targets.affinity: Not reliably characterised per-compound in verifiable literature; notably, oxymetholone has relatively LOW AR-binding affinity yet high anabolic potency, indicating its effect is not affinity-driven alone. Drostanolone and methenolone are moderate-affinity DHT-type agonists.
targets.role: The shared primary target — a nuclear hormone receptor / ligand-activated transcription factor driving muscle protein synthesis and, for oxymetholone, erythropoiesis. Systemic activation also engages hypothalamic-pituitary negative feedback.

targets.action: apparent direct agonist (not via aromatization)
targets.affinity: Not quantified in verifiable sources; inferred from oxymetholone's estrogenic clinical profile despite being non-aromatizable
targets.role: The leading explanation for Anadrol's water retention and gynecomastia — direct ER activation rather than conversion to estradiol

tech.signaling: The androgen receptor is a cytoplasmic nuclear receptor held in an inactive complex with chaperones (e.g. HSP90). Ligand binding releases the chaperones and triggers dimerisation, nuclear translocation, and binding to androgen-response elements, up-regulating genes for protein synthesis (ribosomal biogenesis, IGF-1 signaling, amino-acid transport) and, for oxymetholone, erythropoietin/erythroid genes. The same systemic androgen signal drives hypothalamic-pituitary negative feedback: GnRH, LH and FSH fall, suppressing endogenous testosterone. Oxymetholone adds a second axis by directly engaging estrogen-receptor signaling.

adme.absorb: Oxymetholone and oral methenolone acetate are orally active; oxymetholone's 17α-methyl group specifically enables it to survive hepatic first-pass metabolism. Drostanolone and injectable methenolone enanthate are administered as oil-depot esters intramuscularly.
adme.distribute: Distributes to androgen-receptor-bearing tissues including skeletal muscle, bone marrow, prostate and skin. Injectable esters release slowly from the oil depot, sustaining blood levels over days to weeks depending on ester chain length.
adme.metabolise: Extensive hepatic metabolism. The 17α-alkylation of oxymetholone slows hepatic clearance and underlies its cholestatic hepatotoxicity; injected esters are hydrolysed to the active parent steroid before hepatic metabolism.
adme.excrete: Metabolites are eliminated predominantly in urine; parent and metabolites are detectable for weeks, underpinning WADA anti-doping detection.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker for any of the three. Androgen sensitivity varies generally with AR-gene CAG-repeat length, but this has not been characterised for these molecules, so individual response — including the degree of HPTA suppression — should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: Medical/trial doses are the only ones grounded in evidence and are NOT recommendations: oxymetholone was studied around 50 mg two-to-three times daily in the HIV-wasting trial and is dosed in the low tens of milligrams for anemia. Drostanolone and methenolone physique use is entirely gray-market and unvalidated for safety at any dose. No approved dose exists for muscle-building in healthy people.
tech.tolerance: There is no receptor-downregulation tolerance in the caffeine sense. The dominant time-dependent consequence is cumulative HPTA suppression — falling endogenous testosterone and lowered HDL cholesterol — that builds with continued use and recovers variably after cessation. This suppression, not tachyphylaxis, is what governs on/off cycling in practice.

questions: If it's "selective" for muscle, why does it crash your own testosterone at a dose smaller than a single grain of rice?
Why does the most muscle-building SARM also carry documented cases of a young man's liver shutting down?
How does something with no approved medical use anywhere still have real, placebo-controlled human trials behind it?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Ligandrol binds the androgen receptor almost as tightly as testosterone itself (Ki ≈ 1 nM), so even ~1 mg fully engages the receptor — and your brain reads that androgen signal and shuts down the LH/FSH loop that makes your own testosterone, no matter how "muscle-selective" the drug is. (2) The same trials that showed clean lean-mass gains used tiny 0.1–1 mg doses; real-world users take 10–20× that, and published case reports link those high grey-market doses to cholestatic liver injury — jaundice, itching, elevated enzymes. (3) It was a legitimate pharma candidate (Ligand → Viking's VK5211): a Phase 1 trial in 76 healthy men and a Phase 2 trial in 108 hip-fracture patients both ran and reported real gains — it simply never advanced to a Phase 3 or an approval.

ligandrol-lgd-4033.bigIdea: Ligandrol is a non-steroidal key cut to fit the androgen receptor's muscle-and-bone locks hard while barely turning its prostate-and-skin locks — but it grips that receptor about as tightly as testosterone itself, so "selective" means milder, not clean: a few milligrams still shut down your own testosterone.
ligandrol-lgd-4033.analogy: Testosterone is a master key that opens every androgen lock in your body — muscle, bone, prostate, skin, scalp. Ligandrol is a key filed down to turn the muscle-and-bone locks hard while barely catching the others. Milder side profile, same core machinery — and because it grips the lock about as tightly as the master key, the same alarm still trips: your brain sees a key in the lock and stops making its own.

mechSteps.t: It's a non-steroidal key shaped for one receptor
mechSteps.predict: Steroids are big fatty ring molecules. Ligandrol isn't a steroid at all — so how does it fool the same receptor?
mechSteps.d: LGD-4033 is a non-steroidal molecule that still docks into the androgen receptor with very high affinity (Ki ≈ 0.9–1 nM) — low-nanomolar, in the same ballpark as testosterone or DHT. Same target as a steroid, completely different scaffold.

mechSteps.t: The trick is the shape it forces the receptor into
mechSteps.predict: If it hits the same receptor as testosterone, why doesn't it wreck the prostate and scalp the way steroids do?
mechSteps.d: Because it's non-steroidal, it bends the androgen receptor into a slightly different shape than testosterone does. That shape recruits a different mix of coactivator and corepressor proteins — and muscle and bone carry a different mix than prostate and skin. So the same receptor gets switched strongly ON in muscle and bone and left nearly silent elsewhere. In castrated rats the muscle (levator ani) grows toward ~140% of control while the prostate barely responds.

mechSteps.t: Inside the muscle, it turns genes on
mechSteps.predict: The receptor isn't a channel or a brake — it's a switch that does what, exactly?
mechSteps.d: The activated androgen receptor travels into the cell nucleus and binds DNA, turning up the genes for muscle protein synthesis. This is transcription, not a fast on/off — which is why the effect builds over weeks of dosing rather than minutes, and why lean mass is what actually rises.

mechSteps.t: Your brain reads the signal and pulls back
mechSteps.predict: Your body constantly measures its own androgen level. What does it do when a drug spikes that signal?
mechSteps.d: The hypothalamus and pituitary sense an androgen present and dial down GnRH, LH and FSH — the hormones that tell your testes to make testosterone. Even at 1 mg/day the Phase 1 trial saw dose-dependent drops in total testosterone, SHBG, HDL cholesterol and triglycerides. That's HPTA suppression, and it's the headline cost.

framework.target: The androgen receptor (AR) — a nuclear hormone receptor that acts as a gene switch. Ligandrol is a tissue-selective agonist: it turns AR on (strongly in muscle and bone), rather than blocking it.
framework.affinity: Ki ≈ 0.9–1 nM — genuinely high, low-nanomolar affinity, in the same ballpark as testosterone or DHT. Contrast this with caffeine, where you have to ask whether a realistic dose even reaches the target: here it clearly does, and then some. That near-testosterone affinity is exactly why single-digit-milligram doses fully engage AR — and why suppression of your own testosterone is unavoidable, not a fluke of overdosing. It essentially ignores the progesterone, glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid receptors (Ki > 4,000 nM), so its off-target profile is narrow.
framework.adme: Orally active, cleared by the liver, elimination half-life ≈ 24–36 h — long enough that once-daily dosing keeps it in the blood continuously, and levels roughly triple by day 21 as it accumulates. But note: the felt effect is not set by the blood curve, it's set by weeks of gene transcription.
framework.genetics: No validated Ligandrol-specific pharmacogenetic switch exists. In principle AR sensitivity varies with the AR gene's CAG-repeat length (a general androgen determinant), but this has not been characterised for LGD-4033 specifically — so treat individual response as unpredictable.
framework.evidence: Moderate for a SARM, and honestly better on the function question than most: a Phase 1 trial in healthy young men showed dose-dependent lean-mass gain, and a Phase 2 trial in hip-fracture patients hit its lean-mass endpoint and improved walking distance. But there is no Phase 3, no approval anywhere, and essentially no long-term safety data in healthy people using it to build muscle — alongside published case reports of liver injury.

pk.onset: days to weeks — the anabolic effect is gene transcription, not an acute hit
pk.note: The long half-life supports once-daily dosing and lets blood levels accumulate to roughly triple over three weeks, but don't read it like a stimulant's stopwatch — lean mass accrues over weeks of continuous AR activation, and HPTA suppression builds over the same timescale rather than clearing overnight.

journey.d: You swallow it. Real caution here: grey-market SARM products are frequently mislabeled or contaminated — independent analyses have found the wrong dose, the wrong compound, or an actual banned steroid inside "ligandrol" capsules. It's absorbed across the gut into the blood.
It circulates and distributes to tissues, seeking out androgen receptors. Its ~24–36 h half-life means a daily dose accumulates to a steady, rising level rather than spiking and vanishing.

It binds the androgen receptor and drives it into the nucleus, turning up muscle-protein-synthesis genes. Over weeks this is where lean mass and bone density slowly rise — the effect people are chasing.

The hypothalamus and pituitary detect the androgen signal and throttle back LH and FSH — so your own testosterone production winds down, along with SHBG and HDL cholesterol. This is the suppression that outlasts the last dose.

The liver metabolises it — and in published case reports this is where the worst damage shows: high-dose users developing cholestatic hepatitis, jaundice and itching. In the low-dose trials liver enzymes stayed clean, so this is a dose-and-product story, not an inevitability at 1 mg.

Metabolites leave in urine — and this is why athletes get caught. WADA bans SARMs in and out of competition, and ligandrol is detectable for weeks, producing a long trail of positive doping tests since 2015.

myths.myth: SARMs aren't steroids, so they're basically side-effect-free.
myths.truth: Ligandrol acts on the exact same androgen receptor — and binds it about as tightly as testosterone. It reliably suppresses your own testosterone, lowers HDL cholesterol and SHBG, and has caused documented liver injury. "Not a steroid" describes its chemistry, not its safety.

myths.myth: "Selective" means it only touches muscle.
myths.truth: Selectivity is relative and dose-dependent — it favours muscle and bone over prostate and skin, but the whole-body androgen signal (and therefore HPTA shutdown) is fully intact. The higher the dose, the less selective it behaves.

myths.myth: It's legal and basically a supplement.
myths.truth: No regulator has approved ligandrol for any use. It's sold as a "research chemical," it's banned by WADA and most sports bodies, and the FDA has warned against SARMs in fitness products. Bottle contents are routinely not what the label claims.

myths.myth: The trials proved it's safe.
myths.truth: The trials used 0.1–1 mg/day for 21 days (Phase 1) or up to 2 mg for 12 weeks (Phase 2) under monitoring — and even then testosterone, HDL and SHBG fell dose-dependently. Real-world physique doses are commonly 10–20 mg, far above anything tested for safety, and that's the range the liver-injury case reports come from.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is "the strongest SARM for size — steroid-like lean-mass gains from a pill, with a milder side profile." The lean-mass part has genuine Phase 1 and Phase 2 trial support; the "mild" part is real relative to steroids but oversold.

**The hormonal cost:** it suppresses your own testosterone (HPTA suppression) and lowers HDL cholesterol and SHBG — seen even at 1 mg in trials. Recovery of natural production after stopping is variable and not well characterised, and it scales with the far higher doses people actually take.
**The liver cost:** there are published case reports of drug-induced liver injury — cholestatic hepatitis, jaundice, elevated enzymes — in young men taking high-dose ligandrol products.
**The legal and testing cost:** not approved for any use anywhere; a WADA-banned substance detectable for weeks. If you're a tested athlete, this ends seasons and careers.

**The product-quality cost:** independent analyses repeatedly find grey-market SARMs mislabeled, underdosed, overdosed, or spiked with actual anabolic steroids — so you often don't know what you're taking or at what dose.

**The honest evidence picture:** the human data is short, low-dose Phase 1/2 trials in specific populations — there is essentially no long-term safety data for healthy people using 10–20 mg to build muscle. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a cycle.

start.q: In one line: what does "selective" actually mean for a SARM like ligandrol?
start.a: It favours the muscle and bone locks over the prostate and skin locks — milder, not clean. It still hits the same androgen receptor, about as tightly as testosterone, and still suppresses your own testosterone.

how.q: Ligandrol and a steroid hit the same receptor — so what physically makes ligandrol more tissue-selective?
how.a: Being non-steroidal, it bends the androgen receptor into a different shape, which recruits a different mix of coactivator/corepressor proteins — and muscle/bone carry a different mix than prostate/skin, so it switches AR strongly on in muscle and stays quiet elsewhere.

use.q: Why is HPTA suppression unavoidable even at ~1 mg?
use.a: Its affinity for AR is near-testosterone (Ki ≈ 1 nM), so even a milligram fully engages the receptor — and your hypothalamus/pituitary read that androgen signal and shut down LH/FSH regardless of how "muscle-selective" the drug is. The Phase 1 trial saw testosterone drop dose-dependently at 1 mg.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, really?
evidence.a: Moderate for a SARM: a Phase 1 trial in 76 healthy men and a Phase 2 trial in 108 hip-fracture patients both showed real, dose-dependent lean-mass gains (and the Phase 2 improved walking distance). But there's no Phase 3, no approval anywhere, only low doses were tested, and liver-injury case reports exist at real-world doses.

canExplain: Why a non-steroidal molecule can hit the exact same receptor as testosterone — and grip it nearly as tightly
What "tissue-selective" really means — and why it's relative, not absolute
Why even a muscle-selective drug still shuts down your own testosterone, HDL and SHBG at a 1 mg dose
Why the effect builds over weeks (gene transcription) rather than minutes
Why it showed real gains in human trials yet is approved nowhere — and why athletes test positive for it for weeks

selfTest.q: A friend says ligandrol is safe because "it's not a steroid, it's selective, and it passed human trials." Give the three-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) "Not a steroid" is about chemistry, not safety — it acts on the same androgen receptor and binds it about as tightly as testosterone. (2) "Selective" means it favours muscle/bone, but the whole-body androgen signal is intact, so it still suppresses testosterone, HDL and SHBG. (3) The trials used 0.1–2 mg under monitoring for a few weeks; real-world doses are 10–20 mg, untested for safety and the source of published liver-injury cases.

selfTest.q: The Phase 1 trial gave healthy men just 1 mg/day for three weeks and saw no serious harm — yet case reports describe severe liver injury. What reconciles these?
selfTest.a: Dose and duration. Trial doses were 0.1–1 mg with monitoring; the liver-injury cases involved ~10 mg/day of grey-market product for weeks to months — 10–20× higher, unmonitored, and possibly contaminated. The safety of the trial dose says little about the dose people actually use.

selfTest.q: Why does the ~24–36 h half-life tell you almost nothing about how fast you'll see results?
selfTest.a: Because the effect isn't a blood-level phenomenon like a stimulant — it's driven by the androgen receptor turning genes on over weeks. The half-life sets dosing frequency and explains why blood levels accumulate over three weeks, but muscle accrual (and HPTA suppression) run on the slow transcription timescale, not the clearance clock.

evi.howStrong: For a SARM, ligandrol has some of the strongest human evidence of the class — a completed Phase 1 trial in healthy young men and a completed Phase 2 trial in hip-fracture patients, both randomised and placebo-controlled. But "strong for a SARM" is a low bar: the trials were short (3–12 weeks) and used tiny doses (0.1–2 mg), no Phase 3 was ever run, no regulator has approved it for anything, and there is essentially no long-term safety data in healthy people using it for physique.
evi.whatItShows: Consistently, ligandrol adds lean body mass versus placebo, dose-dependently. Unlike ostarine — which failed its physical-function endpoint — ligandrol's Phase 2 in hip-fracture patients also reported a functional improvement (a modest gain in 6-minute-walk distance), which is a genuinely stronger result. What it has NOT shown is safety or benefit at the doses people actually use, or over any meaningful duration.
evi.effect: In the Phase 1 trial, 1 mg/day for 21 days added roughly ~1.2 kg of lean mass in healthy young men, with dose-dependent drops in testosterone, SHBG, HDL and triglycerides. In the Phase 2 hip-fracture trial, placebo-adjusted lean-mass gains rose with dose (about +4.8%, +7.2% and +9.1% at 0.5, 1 and 2 mg over 12 weeks), alongside fat-mass loss and a >20 m improvement in walking distance at the top dose.
evi.whoBenefits: Studied populations were people losing or needing to preserve muscle: healthy young men (as a model) and older adults recovering from hip fracture. Its use in young healthy people for bodybuilding is entirely off-label, grey-market, and unstudied for safety — and typically at doses 10–20× those tested.
evi.caveats: The trials were short and low-dose; even so, testosterone, HDL and SHBG fell dose-dependently, so HPTA suppression is baked in, not a high-dose surprise. Add documented liver-injury case reports at real-world doses, a WADA ban with weeks-long detection, and grey-market products that are frequently mislabeled or contaminated. Treat the trial numbers as the clean, dosed, monitored best case — not what a capsule off the internet at 10 mg delivers.

trials.finding: Phase 1, 76 healthy young men: LGD-4033 at 0.1, 0.3 and 1 mg/day for 21 days was well tolerated with dose-dependent increases in lean body mass (~1.2 kg at 1 mg) and dose-dependent suppression of total testosterone, SHBG, HDL cholesterol and triglycerides; long half-life with ~3-fold accumulation by day 21.
trials.ref: Basaria et al., 2013, J Gerontol A Biol Sci Med Sci 68(1):87–95

trials.finding: Phase 2 (VK5211), 108 patients recovering from hip fracture: dose-dependent placebo-adjusted increases in lean body mass (~4.8%, 7.2%, 9.1% at 0.5/1/2 mg over 12 weeks), decreased fat mass and improved 6-minute-walk distance, with no drug-related serious adverse events.
trials.ref: Viking Therapeutics VK5211 Phase 2, presented ASBMR 2018 Annual Meeting

trials.finding: Case report: a 32-year-old man developed drug-induced (cholestatic) liver injury after ~2 weeks of 10 mg/day ligandrol taken for muscle building — biopsy-confirmed cholestatic hepatitis with mild fibrosis.
trials.ref: Barbara et al., 2020, ACG Case Reports Journal 7(6):e00370

tech.chem: LGD-4033 (ligandrol; VK5211; CAS 1165910-22-4; PubChem CID 44137675) is a non-steroidal selective androgen receptor modulator — an aryl-propionamide-related pyrrolidinyl-benzonitrile derivative, structurally unrelated to the steroid nucleus of testosterone despite sharing its target. Its non-steroidal scaffold is central to the tissue-selectivity hypothesis: it induces a distinct receptor conformation from that produced by steroidal androgens.

targets.action: tissue-selective agonist
targets.affinity: Ki ≈ 0.9–1 nM (high, low-nanomolar — comparable to testosterone/DHT); in vitro EC50 ≈ 3.6–4.4 nM with near-full efficacy (Emax ~132%)
targets.role: the sole primary target; a nuclear hormone receptor / ligand-activated transcription factor. Anabolic activation in muscle and bone, with minimal stimulation of prostate and skin. Negligible activity at progesterone, glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid receptors (Ki > 4,000 nM).

tech.signaling: The androgen receptor is a nuclear hormone receptor. In the unliganded state it sits in the cytoplasm complexed with chaperones (e.g. HSP90). Ligand binding triggers chaperone release, receptor dimerisation, nuclear translocation, and binding to androgen-response elements in DNA, driving transcription of anabolic genes. Tissue selectivity is attributed to the non-steroidal ligand inducing a receptor conformation that recruits a tissue-specific balance of coactivators and corepressors — strong transcriptional output in muscle/bone, weak in prostate/skin. Systemically, the same androgen signal engages hypothalamic-pituitary negative feedback, suppressing GnRH/LH/FSH and hence endogenous testosterone.

adme.absorb: Orally bioavailable; taken by mouth in trials, with exposure sufficient for once-daily dosing.
adme.distribute: Distributes to androgen-receptor-bearing tissues including skeletal muscle and bone; blood levels accumulate ~3-fold over 21 days of daily dosing due to the long half-life.
adme.metabolise: Hepatic metabolism; characteristic metabolites underpin anti-doping detection assays. High off-label doses are associated in case reports with cholestatic hepatocellular injury.
adme.excrete: Metabolites eliminated in urine (and feces); parent and metabolites detectable in urine for weeks, underpinning WADA testing. Elimination half-life ≈ 24–36 h.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. AR sensitivity varies generally with AR-gene CAG-repeat length, but this has not been characterised for ligandrol response, so individual variation should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: Trial doses were low and monitored: 0.1–1 mg/day in the healthy-men Phase 1, and 0.5–2 mg/day in the Phase 2 hip-fracture program. Grey-market/anecdotal physique use commonly runs far higher (often cited around 5–20 mg/day), at doses never validated for safety and matching the range in the liver-injury case reports — this is a description, not a recommendation. No approved therapeutic dose exists.
tech.tolerance: There is no receptor-downregulation tolerance in the caffeine sense. The dominant time-dependent consequence is cumulative HPTA suppression (falling endogenous testosterone, lowered HDL and SHBG) that builds with continued use and recovers variably after cessation; this — not tachyphylaxis — is what governs "on/off" cycling in practice.

contrasts.title: Ligandrol vs testosterone — filed-down key vs master key
contrasts.point: Same lock (the androgen receptor), opposite reach. Testosterone is a steroid master key that turns every androgen lock — muscle, prostate, skin, scalp — which is why it's powerful and comes with prostate, hair and virilisation effects. Ligandrol is a non-steroidal filed-down key: it turns the muscle-and-bone locks hard and barely catches the others. But it grips the lock about as tightly as the master key (Ki ≈ 1 nM), so the biggest alarm still trips on both — your brain sees an androgen in the lock and shuts down your own production.

contrasts.title: Ligandrol vs ostarine — the stronger SARM, and the better trial result
contrasts.point: Both are non-steroidal SARMs, but ligandrol binds AR harder (Ki ≈ 1 nM vs ostarine's ~3–4 nM) — which is why it's regarded as the stronger mass-builder and correspondingly the stronger suppressor. And where ostarine's Phase 3 failed its physical-function endpoint despite adding muscle, ligandrol's Phase 2 in hip-fracture patients reported both lean-mass and walking-distance gains. That's a genuinely better functional signal — yet ligandrol still has no Phase 3 and no approval, so "better than ostarine" is not "proven safe or effective."

questions: If RAD-140 grips the androgen receptor even harder than testosterone itself, in what sense is it 'selective' or safer?
Why are there published reports of heart-muscle inflammation and severe liver injury — sometimes after just weeks, or even a single dose — from a compound sold as a milder alternative to steroids?
Why is a muscle-building drug being developed as a treatment for breast cancer?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) 'Selective' describes tissue preference, not gentleness: RAD-140 binds AR with very high affinity (Ki ≈ 7 nM — tighter than testosterone's ~29 nM), but favours muscle and bone over prostate and skin. The whole-body androgen signal is fully intact, so it still shuts down your own testosterone. (2) Human safety is barely charted — the only clinical trials are early-phase oncology studies, while the muscle-building use rests on gray-market products, and the real-world harm shows up as case reports: cholestatic liver injury and acute myocarditis, including one report after a first dose. (3) In estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer, switching the androgen receptor ON opposes estrogen-driven growth — the same gene-switching that grows muscle can restrain those tumours, which is the legitimate reason it entered the clinic.

testolone-rad-140.bigIdea: RAD-140 is one of the highest-affinity androgen-receptor agonists ever made — it grabs the same molecular switch as testosterone, harder — and 'selective' only means it favours muscle and bone; the body-wide androgen signal, the testosterone shutdown, and the documented liver and heart harms all come along anyway.
testolone-rad-140.analogy: Testosterone is a master key that turns every androgen lock — muscle, prostate, skin, scalp. RAD-140 is a non-steroidal copy cut to grip the muscle-and-bone lock even tighter than the original, while barely catching the prostate-and-skin locks. Milder side profile in principle, but a firmer grip on the core machinery — and the master key's loudest alarm still trips: your brain sees a key jammed in the lock and stops making its own.

mechSteps.t: A non-steroidal key that grips harder than testosterone
mechSteps.predict: Steroids are fatty ring molecules. RAD-140 isn't a steroid — so how does it fool the same receptor, and how tightly?
mechSteps.d: RAD-140 (testolone) is a non-steroidal molecule that docks into the androgen receptor with very high affinity — Ki ≈ 7 nM, actually tighter than testosterone (~29 nM) or DHT (~10 nM). Same target as a steroid, completely different scaffold, and a firmer grip.

mechSteps.t: The shape it forces on the receptor is the trick
mechSteps.predict: If it hits the same receptor as testosterone, why wouldn't it hammer the prostate and scalp the way steroids do?
mechSteps.d: Being non-steroidal, it bends the androgen receptor into a slightly different shape than testosterone does. That shape recruits a different mix of coactivator and corepressor proteins — and muscle and bone carry a different mix than prostate and skin. So the receptor is switched strongly ON in muscle and bone, and left quieter elsewhere. Unlike testosterone, it also isn't converted to estrogen (no aromatization) or to DHT (no 5α-reduction).

mechSteps.t: Inside the muscle, it turns genes on
mechSteps.predict: The androgen receptor isn't a channel or a brake — it's a switch that does what, exactly?
mechSteps.d: The activated androgen receptor travels into the cell nucleus, binds DNA, and turns up the genes for muscle protein synthesis. This is transcription, not a fast on/off — which is why lean mass builds over weeks of dosing rather than minutes.

mechSteps.t: Your brain reads the signal and pulls back
mechSteps.predict: Your body constantly measures its own androgen level. What does it do when a drug floods that signal?
mechSteps.d: The hypothalamus and pituitary sense an androgen present and dial down GnRH, LH and FSH — the hormones that tell your testes to make testosterone. So even a 'muscle-selective' drug suppresses your own natural production. That's HPTA suppression, and it's the headline hormonal cost.

framework.target: The androgen receptor (AR) — a nuclear hormone receptor that works as a gene switch. RAD-140 is a tissue-selective agonist: it turns AR on (strongly in muscle and bone), rather than blocking it.
framework.affinity: Ki ≈ 7 nM — genuinely high, and notably tighter than testosterone (~29 nM) or DHT (~10 nM). Contrast this with caffeine, where you must ask whether a realistic dose even reaches the target: here it clearly does, and then some. That affinity is exactly why single-digit-milligram doses fully engage AR — and why testosterone suppression is unavoidable, not an artefact of overdosing.
framework.adme: Orally active; in animals bioavailability ran ~27–63% (rat) and ~65–75% (monkey), and it's cleared by the liver. A reliable human elimination half-life isn't well established in the public literature, so treat dosing-interval claims cautiously. Either way, the felt effect is set by weeks of gene transcription, not by a blood curve.
framework.genetics: No validated SARM-specific pharmacogenetic switch exists. AR sensitivity varies generally with the AR gene's CAG-repeat length, but this has never been characterised for RAD-140 specifically — so treat individual response as unpredictable.
framework.evidence: Thin and honest: preclinical animal work shows real anabolic, muscle-favouring activity, and the only human trials are early-phase oncology studies (a first-in-human Phase 1 in ER+/HER2− metastatic breast cancer) focused on safety and dosing — not muscle-building. There is essentially no controlled human evidence, and no long-term safety data, for physique use. What human reports do exist are adverse events: liver injury and myocarditis.

journey.d: You swallow it. Real caution here: gray-market SARM products are frequently mislabeled or contaminated — independent analyses have found the wrong dose, the wrong compound, or an actual banned steroid inside 'RAD-140' capsules. It's absorbed across the gut into the blood.

It circulates and distributes to tissues, seeking out androgen receptors. Because it isn't aromatized to estrogen or reduced to DHT, it engages AR directly rather than being converted into other hormones first.

It binds the androgen receptor with high affinity and drives it into the nucleus, turning up muscle-protein-synthesis genes. Over weeks this is where lean mass and bone density slowly rise — the effect people are chasing.
The hypothalamus and pituitary detect the androgen signal and throttle back LH and FSH — so your own testosterone production winds down. This suppression can outlast the last dose.

The liver metabolises it — and in case reports this is where damage shows: cholestatic drug-induced liver injury with deep jaundice. Separately, there are published reports of acute myocarditis (heart-muscle inflammation), in one case after a first dose.

Metabolites leave in urine — and this is why athletes get caught. WADA bans SARMs in and out of competition; RAD-140 and its metabolites are detectable long after use, producing positive doping tests.

myths.myth: It binds the receptor even tighter than testosterone, so it's both stronger and safer.
myths.truth: Higher affinity is not safety — it's the opposite of gentle. Tighter binding is exactly why low doses fully engage AR and why suppression is guaranteed. 'Selective' refers to which tissues respond, not to whether it harms you.

myths.myth: 'Selective' means it only touches muscle.
myths.truth: Selectivity is relative and dose-dependent. It favours muscle and bone over prostate and skin, but the whole-body androgen signal — and therefore the HPTA shutdown — is fully intact. The higher the dose, the less selective it behaves.

myths.myth: SARMs aren't steroids, so they're basically side-effect-free.
myths.truth: RAD-140 acts on the very same androgen receptor, and 'not a steroid' describes its chemistry, not its safety. It suppresses your own testosterone, and there are documented case reports of severe cholestatic liver injury and acute myocarditis linked to its use.

myths.myth: It's been through human trials, so it's proven to work and be safe for building muscle.
myths.truth: The only human studies are early-phase oncology trials (safety and dosing in breast cancer patients). There are no controlled trials of RAD-140 for muscle-building in healthy people, and no long-term safety data — the physique use is entirely off-label and gray-market.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is 'steroid-strong lean-mass gains with fewer steroid side effects, in a pill.' The animal data on anabolism is real, and the receptor affinity is genuinely high — but the 'fewer side effects' half is unproven in humans and undercut by the case reports.

**The hormonal cost:** as a high-affinity AR agonist, it suppresses your own testosterone (HPTA suppression). Recovery of natural production after stopping is variable and not well characterised for this compound.

**The liver cost:** there are published case reports of severe cholestatic drug-induced liver injury from RAD-140 used for body-building — deep jaundice, weeks-long illness (Ochsner Journal 2022; Australian Prescriber 2024).

**The cardiac cost:** there are published case reports of acute myocarditis and myopericarditis associated with RAD-140, including one reported after a single first dose — a rare but serious signal for a physique supplement.
**The legal and testing cost:** not approved for any use anywhere; a WADA-banned substance detectable long after use. For a tested athlete, this ends seasons and careers.

**The product-quality cost:** independent analyses repeatedly find gray-market SARMs mislabeled, underdosed, overdosed, or spiked with actual anabolic steroids — so you often don't know what's in the bottle.

**The honest evidence picture:** human data is limited to early-phase oncology trials plus a scatter of adverse-event case reports — there are no controlled muscle-building trials and no long-term safety data in healthy people. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a cycle.

start.q: In one line: what does 'selective' actually mean for RAD-140 — and does it mean 'safe'?
start.a: It means it favours the muscle-and-bone locks over the prostate-and-skin locks — a tissue preference, not gentleness. It still hits the same androgen receptor (in fact harder than testosterone) and still suppresses your own testosterone.

how.q: RAD-140 and a steroid hit the same receptor — so what physically makes RAD-140 more tissue-selective?
how.a: Being non-steroidal, it bends the androgen receptor into a different shape, which recruits a different mix of coactivator/corepressor proteins — and muscle/bone carry a different mix than prostate/skin, so AR switches strongly on in muscle and stays quieter elsewhere. It also isn't converted to estrogen or DHT.

use.q: Why is testosterone suppression unavoidable even at low doses?
use.a: Its affinity for AR is very high (Ki ≈ 7 nM, tighter than testosterone), so even a few milligrams fully engages the receptor — and your hypothalamus and pituitary read that androgen signal and shut down LH/FSH regardless of how 'muscle-selective' the drug is.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence, really?
evidence.a: Weak and one-sided: the muscle-favouring anabolic effect is shown mainly in animals, the only human trials are early-phase oncology (breast cancer) safety studies, and the human 'literature' for physique use is largely adverse-event case reports — liver injury and myocarditis. No controlled muscle-building trials, no long-term safety data.

canExplain: Why a non-steroidal molecule can hit the exact same receptor as testosterone — and bind it even more tightly
What 'tissue-selective' really means, and why higher affinity is a warning sign rather than a safety feature
Why even a muscle-selective drug still shuts down your own testosterone
Why the effect builds over weeks (gene transcription) rather than minutes
Why the human evidence is thin — early-phase oncology trials plus liver- and heart-injury case reports — not muscle-building proof

selfTest.q: A friend says RAD-140 is safer than steroids because 'it binds even harder and it's selective.' Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) Binding harder is not safer — high affinity (Ki ≈ 7 nM) is exactly why low doses fully engage AR and why testosterone suppression is guaranteed. (2) 'Selective' means it favours muscle/bone over prostate/skin, but the whole-body androgen signal is intact — and there are documented case reports of severe liver injury and acute myocarditis.

selfTest.q: Why does the fact that RAD-140 isn't aromatized or 5α-reduced NOT make it side-effect-free?
selfTest.a: Skipping conversion to estrogen and DHT changes the side-effect flavour (less estrogenic and DHT-mediated activity), but the drug still directly activates the androgen receptor throughout the body — so HPTA suppression, lowered testosterone, and the reported liver and cardiac harms remain.

selfTest.q: Someone points out RAD-140 has 'been in human clinical trials.' Why doesn't that validate using it to build muscle?
selfTest.a: Those are early-phase oncology trials (safety and dosing in ER+/HER2− breast cancer), where activating AR opposes estrogen-driven tumour growth. They weren't muscle-building studies, they don't establish efficacy or long-term safety in healthy people, and no regulator has approved the drug for any use.

evi.howStrong: Weak human evidence, and lopsided. The anabolic, muscle-favouring activity of RAD-140 rests mainly on preclinical work in rodents and monkeys (its original design paper), which is genuinely well-characterised chemistry — but animal data. In humans, the only clinical trials are early-phase oncology studies: a first-in-human Phase 1 in ER+/HER2− metastatic breast cancer designed to test safety and dosing, not muscle-building. There are no controlled trials of RAD-140 for physique in healthy people, and no long-term safety data. The human literature that does exist for that use is a scatter of adverse-event case reports.
evi.whatItShows: Preclinically, RAD-140 reliably stimulates muscle and bone while sparing the prostate relative to testosterone — the tissue-selectivity it was designed for. In humans, what's actually documented is (a) that it can be dosed in cancer patients under monitoring, and (b) that unmonitored use has caused serious organ injury. It has NOT been shown in any controlled human trial to safely build muscle in healthy people.
evi.effect: There's no reliable human effect size for muscle-building, because the trials that would produce one don't exist. Its receptor affinity (Ki ≈ 7 nM) is higher than testosterone's, which predicts potent anabolic signalling — but 'predicts' from animals and biochemistry is not the same as a measured human result.
evi.whoBenefits: The studied population is breast cancer patients, where AR activation is the therapeutic point. Use by young healthy people for bodybuilding is entirely off-label, gray-market, and unstudied for safety — the group most likely to use it is the group with the least evidence behind them.
evi.caveats: Take the harm signals seriously: published case reports link RAD-140 to severe cholestatic drug-induced liver injury and to acute myocarditis/myopericarditis, one reported after a single first dose. Add near-certain HPTA suppression, WADA prohibition with long detection windows, and gray-market products that are routinely mislabeled or spiked. Case reports can't establish how common these harms are — but they prove the harms are real, and there is no counterweight of controlled safety data.

trials.finding: Design and preclinical characterization: RAD140 binds the androgen receptor with high affinity (Ki ≈ 7 nM vs ~29 nM testosterone, ~10 nM DHT) and shows tissue-selective anabolic activity (muscle/bone favoured over prostate) in rodents and non-human primates.
trials.ref: Miller et al., 2011, ACS Medicinal Chemistry Letters

trials.finding: First-in-human Phase 1 dose-escalation study of RAD140 in ER+/HER2− metastatic breast cancer — an early safety and pharmacokinetic study, not a muscle-building trial (NCT03088527).
trials.ref: Phase 1 abstract P5-11-01, AACR 2020, Cancer Research 80(4 Suppl)

trials.finding: Case report: acute myocarditis (elevated troponin, reduced ejection fraction, no obstructive coronary disease) in a 32-year-old man after self-administering RAD-140 for muscle gain — described as the first reported SARM-associated myocarditis.
trials.ref: Padappayil et al., 2022, Cureus

trials.finding: Case report: myopericarditis in a 16-year-old following use of RAD-140, reported after the first dose.
trials.ref: 2024, JACC: Case Reports

trials.finding: Case report: severe cholestatic drug-induced liver injury with marked jaundice in a young man using RAD-140 for body-building.
trials.ref: Australian Prescriber, 2024 (and RAD-140 Drug-Induced Liver Injury, Ochsner Journal, 2022)

tech.chem: RAD-140 (testolone; also designated vosilasarm; CAS 1182367-47-0) is a non-steroidal selective androgen receptor modulator, structurally unrelated to the steroid nucleus of testosterone despite sharing its target. Its non-steroidal scaffold is central to the tissue-selectivity hypothesis: it induces a distinct androgen-receptor conformation from that produced by steroidal androgens. Unlike testosterone, it is not a substrate for aromatase (no conversion to estrogen) or 5α-reductase (no conversion to DHT).

targets.action: tissue-selective agonist
targets.affinity: Ki ≈ 7 nM — higher affinity than testosterone (~29 nM) or DHT (~10 nM); a potent AR agonist
targets.role: the sole primary target; a nuclear hormone receptor / ligand-activated transcription factor. Anabolic activation in muscle and bone, with attenuated stimulation of prostate and skin. In ER+ breast cancer cells, AR activation opposes estrogen-driven proliferation — the oncology rationale.

tech.signaling: The androgen receptor is a nuclear hormone receptor. Unliganded, it sits in the cytoplasm complexed with chaperones (e.g. HSP90). Ligand binding triggers chaperone release, receptor dimerisation, nuclear translocation, and binding to androgen-response elements in DNA, driving transcription of anabolic genes. Tissue selectivity is attributed to the non-steroidal ligand inducing a receptor conformation that recruits a tissue-specific balance of coactivators and corepressors — strong transcriptional output in muscle/bone, weaker in prostate/skin. Systemically, the same androgen signal engages hypothalamic–pituitary negative feedback, suppressing GnRH/LH/FSH and hence endogenous testosterone.

adme.absorb: Orally active; preclinical oral bioavailability ~27–63% (rat) and ~65–75% (monkey).
adme.distribute: Distributes to androgen-receptor-bearing tissues including skeletal muscle and bone; not aromatized or 5α-reduced, so it engages AR directly rather than via active metabolites.
adme.metabolise: Hepatic metabolism; the liver is also the site of the documented drug-induced (cholestatic) liver injury in case reports. A reliable human elimination half-life is not well established in the public literature.
adme.excrete: Metabolites eliminated in urine; parent and metabolites detectable well after use, underpinning WADA anti-doping detection.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. AR sensitivity varies generally with AR-gene CAG-repeat length, but this has not been characterised for RAD-140 response, so individual variation should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: No approved therapeutic dose exists. Early-phase oncology trials used low, monitored oral doses in a dose-escalation design. Gray-market/anecdotal physique use is commonly cited around 10–20 mg/day — doses never validated for safety, and delivered by products of unverified content. This is a description, not a recommendation.
tech.tolerance: There is no receptor-downregulation tolerance in the caffeine sense. The dominant time-dependent consequence is cumulative HPTA suppression (falling endogenous testosterone) that builds with continued use and recovers variably after cessation; this — plus the risk of organ injury seen in case reports — is what actually governs the harm profile, not tachyphylaxis.

questions: Why does one of these compounds literally tint your vision yellow — what does a muscle drug have to do with your eyes?
How does YK-11 claim to push muscle growth past the limit every other SARM stops at?
Why was one of these three studied as a male birth-control pill rather than a muscle drug?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) Andarine reaches androgen receptors in the eye as well as in muscle, and at higher doses this disturbs the retina's light response — producing a reversible yellow-green tint and poor night vision that fades once you stop. (2) YK-11 does something no ordinary SARM does: on top of switching the androgen receptor on, it makes muscle cells produce more follistatin, which neutralises myostatin — the body's built-in brake on muscle size. Crucially, that finding comes only from cells in a dish, never a human. (3) S-23 is the most potent and most suppressive of the three (Ki ≈ 1.7 nM, a full agonist); in rats it shut down LH and FSH so completely that it caused reversible azoospermia — which is exactly why Dalton's group studied it as a contraceptive, not a physique drug.

andarine-s4-yk-11-s-23.bigIdea: These three are the experimental deep end of the SARM shelf: each takes the same androgen-receptor engine and cranks one dial to an extreme — Andarine tints your vision, YK-11 releases the myostatin brake, S-23 maxes out potency and testosterone shutdown — and all three rest on far thinner human evidence than even ostarine, which is to say essentially none.
andarine-s4-yk-11-s-23.analogy: If ostarine is the entry-level SARM, picture these three as prototypes pulled off the same engine block, each with one dial cranked past the safe zone and none of them road-legal. Same core machinery — a key cut for the androgen receptor — but Andarine's key also rattles the lock in your eye, YK-11's key trips a second circuit that cuts the muscle-growth brake, and S-23's key is filed sharpest of all, turning every lock hardest, including the one that switches your own testosterone off.

mechSteps.t: All three key into the same receptor
mechSteps.predict: Ostarine, testosterone and these three all converge on one protein. Which receptor — and is it a channel, a brake, or a switch?
mechSteps.d: Every one of these compounds docks into the androgen receptor, a nuclear hormone receptor that acts as a gene switch. S-23 and Andarine are non-steroidal (aryl-propionamides, like ostarine); YK-11 is unusual — it's a steroidal molecule built on a DHT-like scaffold. Different shapes, same lock.

mechSteps.t: The switch turns genes on over weeks
mechSteps.predict: If the receptor is a gene switch rather than a fast on/off, how quickly should you expect to feel anything?
mechSteps.d: An activated androgen receptor travels into the nucleus, binds DNA, and turns up muscle- and bone-building genes. This is transcription, not an acute hit — so any anabolic effect builds over weeks of continuous dosing, and lean mass and bone are what slowly rise.

mechSteps.t: Andarine's quirk: it reaches the eye
mechSteps.predict: Androgen receptors aren't only in muscle. If Andarine reaches the ones in your eye, what might you actually see?
mechSteps.d: Andarine (S4) engages androgen receptors beyond muscle, including in the eye, where at higher doses it disturbs how the retina handles light. The well-known result is a yellow-green tint to vision and trouble adjusting to darkness — dose-dependent and fully reversible on stopping. It's the clearest proof that 'selective' never meant 'muscle-only'.

mechSteps.t: YK-11's extra trick: it cuts the muscle brake
mechSteps.predict: Your body caps muscle growth with a protein called myostatin. What would happen if a drug quietly disabled that cap?
mechSteps.d: In cell studies, YK-11 does more than switch the androgen receptor on — it drives muscle cells to make follistatin, which mops up myostatin (GDF-8), the signal that normally limits how big a muscle fibre gets. Release that brake and the growth ceiling lifts. Read the honest caveat carefully: this has only ever been shown in cultured cells, never in a living animal or a person.

mechSteps.t: S-23's extreme: full shutdown of your own hormones
mechSteps.predict: S-23 binds the receptor hardest of the three. What does your brain do the moment it senses that strong androgen signal?
mechSteps.d: S-23 is the most potent (Ki ≈ 1.7 nM) and behaves as a full agonist. Your hypothalamus and pituitary read that powerful androgen signal and slam the brakes on GnRH, LH and FSH — the hormones that tell your testes to make testosterone and sperm. In rats this suppression was complete enough to cause reversible azoospermia, which is why it was tested as a male contraceptive. Every SARM does this; S-23 just does it hardest.

framework.target: The androgen receptor (AR) — a nuclear hormone receptor that works as a gene switch — is the shared target of all three, and all three are agonists (they turn it on, not block it). YK-11 carries a second string: in cell models it also raises follistatin, indirectly disinhibiting muscle growth by neutralising myostatin. So YK-11 is best read as AR agonist plus a myostatin-pathway modulator.
framework.affinity: All three bind AR in the low-nanomolar range, so single-digit-milligram doses fully engage the target — this is a real target genuinely hit, not a caffeine-style 'does the dose even reach it'. S-23 is the tightest binder (Ki ≈ 1.7 nM, a full agonist — near testosterone's own affinity), Andarine sits a little weaker (roughly Ki 4–8 nM depending on the assay, and a partial agonist in some tissues). Because affinity is this high, HPTA suppression is baked in, not a fluke of overdosing.
framework.adme: All three are orally active. Be blunt about what's missing: human pharmacokinetics are essentially uncharacterised for all three. The one solid PK number — S-23's ~12 h half-life and ~96% oral bioavailability — comes from rats, not people, so don't treat it as a human dosing clock. And as with any AR agonist, the felt effect isn't set by the blood curve anyway; it's set by weeks of gene transcription.
framework.genetics: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic switch exists for any of the three — nothing like caffeine's CYP1A2. AR sensitivity varies generally with the AR gene's CAG-repeat length, but that has never been characterised for these molecules, so individual response should be treated as unpredictable.
framework.evidence: This is the whole point of the page, and it's thin. Andarine: animal studies only (bone and body-composition work in rats), no completed human efficacy trials. YK-11: cell-culture only — zero human data, and not even a solid in-vivo animal muscle study. S-23: rat contraceptive and tissue-selectivity studies, zero human data. None is approved by any regulator for anything, and all are banned by WADA.

journey.d: You swallow it — and this is the first real hazard, before any pharmacology. Independent testing repeatedly finds gray-market SARMs mislabeled, underdosed, overdosed, or spiked with actual anabolic steroids. With YK-11 and S-23 especially — obscure compounds with tiny markets — you often can't be sure what's in the bottle. It's absorbed across the gut into the blood.

It circulates and distributes to androgen-receptor-bearing tissues. S-23's rat data suggest good oral absorption and a half-life long enough for once-daily dosing, but remember that's rat PK — the human numbers simply haven't been measured.

The compound binds the androgen receptor, drives it into the nucleus, and turns up muscle- and bone-building genes over weeks. This is where YK-11's extra claimed action lives too: in cultured muscle cells it raises follistatin to neutralise myostatin — the effect people chase, shown only in a dish.

Unique to Andarine: it reaches androgen receptors in the eye and, at higher doses, disturbs the retina's handling of light. You may see a yellow-green tint and struggle in the dark. It's dose-dependent and reverses when you stop — but it's a vivid reminder that 'selective' is relative.

The hypothalamus and pituitary detect the androgen signal and throttle back LH and FSH, winding down your own testosterone. S-23 does this hardest of the three — hard enough, in rats, to stop sperm production entirely. This suppression outlasts the last dose, and recovery is variable and poorly characterised.

Metabolites leave in urine — and this is why athletes get caught. WADA bans SARMs in and out of competition, and these compounds and their metabolites are detectable for weeks, producing a long trail of positive doping tests.

myths.myth: SARMs aren't steroids, so they're basically side-effect-free.
myths.truth: They act on the same androgen receptor and reliably suppress your own testosterone. The 'not a steroid' line is also just wrong for YK-11, which is a steroidal molecule built on a DHT-like scaffold. Chemistry is not safety.

myths.myth: 'Selective' means it only touches muscle.
myths.truth: Andarine is the living counterexample — it reaches androgen receptors in the eye and can tint your vision. Selectivity is relative and dose-dependent, and the whole-body androgen signal (and therefore hormonal shutdown) stays fully intact.

myths.myth: YK-11 is a proven myostatin inhibitor that beats normal SARMs.
myths.truth: The follistatin/myostatin finding is real but comes entirely from cultured cells (Kanno's C2C12 work). There is no human data, and not even a solid living-animal study showing it grows muscle. 'Proven' is far too strong a word for a dish-only result.

myths.myth: They're legal research chemicals, basically supplements.
myths.truth: None is approved for any use anywhere. All are WADA-banned, the FDA has warned against SARMs in fitness products, and S-23 was developed as an experimental contraceptive — not a physique aid. Bottle contents are routinely not what the label claims.

whenToUse.intro: Why people reach for them — and what they cost:
items: **The pitch, compound by compound:** Andarine is sold for 'hardening' and recomposition; YK-11 for breaking past a supposed genetic muscle ceiling via myostatin; S-23 as 'the strongest SARM'. The common thread is a promise of steroid-like results with fewer downsides — a promise the human evidence cannot back up for any of the three.

**The hormonal cost is the headline:** all three suppress your own testosterone, and S-23 is contraceptive-grade — in rats it shut down sperm production completely. Recovery after stopping is variable and poorly characterised in humans because it's barely been studied in humans at all.
**Andarine's signature cost:** a dose-dependent yellow-green tint to vision and impaired night vision. It's reversible, but it's a clear signal the drug is acting well outside muscle.

**YK-11's unknown-unknowns:** it's steroidal, has zero human data, and its marquee mechanism has only ever been seen in cells. Injectable gray-market versions add infection and dosing risks on top of a molecule no one has safely characterised.
**The legal and testing cost:** not approved for any use, WADA-banned, and detectable for weeks. For a tested athlete this ends seasons and careers.

**The product-quality cost:** independent analyses repeatedly find these compounds mislabeled, underdosed, overdosed, or spiked with real steroids — so you frequently don't know what you're actually taking.

**The honest evidence picture:** Andarine is animal-only, YK-11 is cell-culture-only, S-23 is rat-only. There is essentially no human data on any of the three for building muscle. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a cycle.

start.q: In one line: what do Andarine, YK-11 and S-23 have in common, and how are they the 'deep end'?
start.a: All three switch on the same androgen receptor, but each pushes one dial to an extreme (vision, myostatin, suppression) — and all rest on far thinner human evidence than ostarine: essentially none.

how.q: What makes YK-11 mechanistically different from a plain SARM like ostarine?
how.a: On top of activating the androgen receptor, YK-11 raises follistatin in muscle cells, which neutralises myostatin — the brake on muscle growth. But this is a cell-culture finding only, with no human confirmation.

use.q: Why is S-23 the one that gets singled out for the harshest hormonal warning?
use.a: It's the most potent (Ki ≈ 1.7 nM) full agonist of the three, so it suppresses LH and FSH hardest — enough in rats to cause reversible azoospermia, which is why it was studied as a male contraceptive.

evidence.q: Rank the three by how much human evidence exists.
evidence.a: It's a rank among near-zeros: none has human efficacy data. Andarine has animal (rat) studies, S-23 has rat contraceptive studies, and YK-11 has only cultured-cell studies. None is approved anywhere.

canExplain: Why all three act on the same androgen receptor as testosterone, despite YK-11 being steroidal and the other two not
Why Andarine can tint your vision yellow — and why that disproves the idea that 'selective' means muscle-only
What YK-11's myostatin/follistatin mechanism is, and why 'proven in cells' is not 'proven in people'
Why S-23 was studied as a male contraceptive, and why every SARM suppresses your own testosterone
Why the human evidence for all three is essentially nonexistent — animal for S4, cell-culture for YK-11, rat-only for S-23

selfTest.q: A friend says YK-11 is better than ostarine because 'it inhibits myostatin, which has been proven to build extra muscle.' Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) The mechanism claim is fair in outline — YK-11 raises follistatin, which neutralises myostatin, the muscle-growth brake. (2) But 'proven to build extra muscle' is wrong: that finding exists only in cultured cells (Kanno's C2C12 work), with no human and not even a solid living-animal study.
