Add near-universal fluid retention, glucose intolerance, joint and nerve symptoms, acromegalic changes with chronic use, a real tumour-growth concern, prescription/legal restriction, and grey-market products that are frequently counterfeit or contaminated. Trial numbers reflect clean, dosed, monitored conditions — not a black-market vial.

trials.finding: 21 healthy men aged 61–81 given GH (~0.03 mg/kg 3×/week) for 6 months gained ~4.7 kg lean body mass and lost ~3.5 kg fat mass, with increased lumbar-spine density — but also rising systolic blood pressure and fasting glucose. The trial that launched the anti-aging hype.
trials.ref: Rudman et al., 1990, N Engl J Med 323:1–6

trials.finding: Randomised controlled trial in healthy aged men and women: GH increased lean mass and decreased fat mass but did NOT improve muscle strength or VO2max, and adverse events — especially glucose intolerance — were frequent.
trials.ref: Blackman et al., 2002, JAMA 288:2282–2292

trials.finding: Systematic review of GH in the healthy elderly: only small body-composition changes and increased rates of adverse events; concluded GH cannot be recommended as an anti-aging therapy.
trials.ref: Liu et al., 2007, Ann Intern Med 146:104–115

trials.finding: Systematic review of GH and athletic performance: GH did not improve strength or aerobic capacity in healthy young adults and increased adverse events (notably edema and fatigue).
trials.ref: Liu et al., 2008, Ann Intern Med 148:747–758

tech.chem: Somatropin is recombinant human growth hormone — a 191-amino-acid, ~22 kDa single-chain polypeptide with two intramolecular disulfide bonds, sequence-identical to pituitary-derived hGH and produced in E. coli or mammalian cell lines. It is a protein hormone of the somatotropin/prolactin cytokine family, not a steroid; it cannot cross membranes and acts entirely through a cell-surface receptor.

targets.action: full agonist — one GH crosslinks a pre-formed receptor dimer via two non-equivalent binding sites, driving the conformational change that activates receptor-associated JAK2
targets.affinity: high: site 1 sub-nanomolar, site 2 Kd ≈ 4 nM — fully engaged at therapeutic exposure
targets.role: the sole primary target; a single-pass class-I cytokine receptor. Signals through JAK2/STAT5 to drive lipolysis, protein synthesis, collagen turnover and hepatic IGF-1 output

tech.signaling: GHR is a class-I cytokine receptor with no intrinsic kinase activity; it pre-associates as a dimer with JAK2 bound to each intracellular tail. GH binding rotates/repositions the transmembrane helices so the two JAK2 molecules transactivate one another. Active JAK2 phosphorylates receptor tyrosines that recruit STAT5 (and STAT1/3); phosphorylated STAT5 dimerises, translocates to the nucleus, and transcribes target genes including IGF1. Parallel arms include the MAPK/ERK and PI3K/AKT pathways. Systemically, GH and IGF-1 feed back on the hypothalamus and pituitary (↑somatostatin, ↓GHRH drive), suppressing endogenous GH pulses during exogenous dosing.

adme.absorb: Subcutaneous injection with roughly 80% bioavailability; slow release from the injection depot governs the blood-level time course.
adme.distribute: Distributes to GHR-bearing tissues (liver, muscle, bone, adipose); as a large hydrophilic protein it stays extracellular and acts at the cell surface.
adme.metabolise: Cleared by receptor-mediated uptake and proteolytic degradation, chiefly in the liver and kidney; broken down to amino acids rather than to discrete drug metabolites. Metabolic half-life ~20–30 min; observed subcutaneous half-life ~2–4 h.
adme.excrete: Minimal intact renal excretion — elimination is via tissue catabolism, not urinary excretion of unchanged hormone.

tech.pgx: No validated single-gene switch governing response. The GHR exon-3 deletion polymorphism (d3-GHR) has been associated in some pediatric cohorts with a greater growth response to GH therapy, but results are inconsistent across studies and populations, so it is not a reliable predictor. IGF-1 pathway variation may modulate response but is not clinically actionable here.
tech.dose: Therapeutic dosing is weight- or condition-based and physician-titrated to IGF-1 levels — adult deficiency typically uses small daily microgram-per-kg subcutaneous doses. Historical study doses like Rudman's ~0.03 mg/kg three times weekly are now considered high. Grey-market physique use runs well above replacement, at exposures never validated for safety — a description, not a recommendation. No approved dose exists for anti-aging or athletic use.
tech.tolerance: No receptor-desensitisation tolerance in the caffeine sense. The dominant time-dependent phenomena are (1) suppression of endogenous GH pulses during dosing via negative feedback, and (2) cumulative metabolic and structural consequences of sustained supraphysiologic signalling — insulin resistance, fluid retention, and acromegalic tissue changes — that build with continued use rather than fading.

questions: Growth hormone is the famous muscle hormone — so why would anyone inject IGF-1 instead of GH?
What does the cryptic "LR3" in the name actually change — and why does that tiny edit make it far more potent than the IGF-1 your own body makes?
If it builds muscle by telling cells to grow, what stops it from telling a tumour the exact same thing?
hook.payoff: You can now answer all three. (1) GH barely touches muscle directly — it mostly works by ordering your liver and muscle to produce IGF-1, and IGF-1 is the molecule that actually drives the growth; injecting IGF-1 LR3 skips the middleman entirely. (2) "LR3" = Long R3: a 13-amino-acid N-terminal extension plus an arginine swapped in at position 3. That edit drops the molecule's grip on the IGF-binding proteins roughly 1,000-fold — and since those proteins normally keep about 99% of your natural IGF-1 leashed and inactive, a version that dodges them stays free and active far longer. (3) Nothing stops it — that is the core danger. IGF1R sits on almost every cell in your body, so the same "grow and survive" command that thickens muscle also feeds any tumour carrying the receptor, which is why circulating IGF-1 levels track with cancer risk in population studies.

igf-1-lr3.bigIdea: IGF-1 LR3 is the growth signal GH actually works through, re-engineered to slip past the binding proteins that normally keep it leashed — so it floods the IGF-1 receptor continuously, telling muscle to grow but telling every other tissue, tumours included, the exact same thing.
igf-1-lr3.analogy: Think of GH as a foreman shouting \"grow\" and IGF-1 as the actual work order it hands to the cell. Your natural IGF-1 comes chaperoned — binding proteins hold ~99% of it in reserve, releasing only a trickle. IGF-1 LR3 is that same work order with the chaperone cut loose and the paper laminated so it can't be shredded: it stays active far longer, and it reaches everywhere, not just the muscle you meant.

mechSteps.t: It's GH's real messenger, made injectable
mechSteps.predict: GH is the famous muscle hormone — yet GH barely acts on muscle directly. So what molecule actually does the growing?
mechSteps.d: Growth hormone works largely by instructing your liver and muscle to make insulin-like growth factor-1. IGF-1 is the downstream signal that truly drives tissue growth. IGF-1 LR3 is a lab-made IGF-1 analog injected directly, cutting GH out of the loop.

mechSteps.t: The "LR3" edits cut the leash
mechSteps.predict: Your natural IGF-1 is almost entirely bound to carrier proteins that keep it inactive. What happens if you engineer a version those proteins can't grab?
mechSteps.d: Native IGF-1 is 70 amino acids; LR3 is 83 — a 13-amino-acid N-terminal extension plus an arginine substituted at position 3. Those changes lower its affinity for the IGF-binding proteins roughly 1,000-fold, so it escapes the reservoir that normally sequesters and clears IGF-1 — and stays free and active far longer.

mechSteps.t: At the receptor, it switches on the growth machinery
mechSteps.predict: IGF1R is a receptor tyrosine kinase — a gene-level switch. Name the two things it turns up inside a muscle cell.
mechSteps.d: Bound IGF-1 activates IGF1R, firing the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway (ramping up muscle protein synthesis) and the Ras/MAPK pathway (proliferation). It also activates satellite cells — the muscle's stem cells — the route by which IGF-1 can, in models, add new muscle nuclei and fibres (hyperplasia).

mechSteps.t: It also looks enough like insulin to crash your blood sugar
mechSteps.predict: IGF-1 and insulin are molecular cousins built on the same scaffold. What dangerous side effect does that shared shape create?
mechSteps.d: Because IGF-1 resembles insulin, it binds the insulin receptor with low affinity and drives glucose out of the blood into cells while suppressing the liver's glucose output. Push the dose and blood sugar can fall dangerously — hypoglycemia is the most immediate, real risk.

mechSteps.t: The "grow" signal doesn't discriminate
mechSteps.predict: If the message is "divide and survive" to any cell carrying the receptor, what is the long-term worry?
mechSteps.d: IGF1R sits on nearly every tissue. The same pro-growth, anti-apoptosis signal that thickens muscle also stimulates organs (heart, spleen, kidney can enlarge) and feeds any tumour expressing the receptor. Higher circulating IGF-1 is associated with elevated risk of several cancers in epidemiological studies.

framework.target: The IGF-1 receptor (IGF1R) — a receptor tyrosine kinase that acts as a growth switch. IGF-1 LR3 is an agonist: it turns IGF1R on, hard and continuously, rather than blocking anything. It also spills onto the closely related insulin receptor at low affinity.
framework.affinity: The LR3 edits were designed to leave IGF1R binding essentially intact — LR3 retains high (nanomolar-range) affinity for its receptor, comparable to native IGF-1. What the modifications drastically changed is affinity for the IGF-binding proteins (~1,000-fold lower). So unlike caffeine — where you must ask whether a realistic dose even reaches the target — here the target is fully and continuously engaged; that is precisely why the growth signal is so hard to contain.
framework.adme: Injected (a peptide, destroyed if swallowed), typically subcutaneously. Its defining feature is extended action: by evading the binding proteins that normally sequester and clear IGF-1, it circulates free for much longer. A half-life in the ~20–30 hour range is widely cited, but that figure comes from gray-market and preclinical sources, not rigorous human pharmacokinetic studies — treat it as approximate, not established.
framework.genetics: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic switch. Individual response is shaped by baseline IGF-1/IGFBP levels, IGF1R expression and insulin sensitivity — all variable — so the effect (and the hypoglycemia risk) is genuinely unpredictable person to person.
framework.evidence: Essentially absent for this use. IGF-1 LR3 (as LONG R3 IGF-I) is manufactured and sold as a cell-culture reagent, not a human drug. There are no human trials of injected IGF-1 LR3 for muscle building, and no long-term human safety data. The only approved IGF-1 product is native rhIGF-1 (mecasermin), and only for severe primary IGF-1 deficiency — a completely different context.

journey.d: It's injected, usually under the skin — swallowing would digest it. Real caution: gray-market peptides are frequently mislabeled, underdosed or contaminated, so what's in the vial often isn't what the label claims.

In circulation it does what native IGF-1 can't: it slips past the IGF-binding proteins that normally grab ~99% of IGF-1 and hold it in reserve. Staying free and undegraded is exactly why it lingers and acts for so long.

It docks onto IGF1R and fires the Akt/mTOR growth machinery, ramping up protein synthesis and waking satellite cells. Over days-to-weeks of signalling, this is the muscle-growth effect people are chasing.

Its insulin-like shape lets it hit the insulin receptor too, pulling glucose out of the blood and shutting down the liver's glucose output. This is where the acute danger lives — blood sugar can drop hard enough to be an emergency.

IGF1R is nearly everywhere, so the "grow and survive" signal isn't confined to muscle. Organs can enlarge, and any tumour bearing the receptor gets the same growth message — the mechanistic basis of the cancer concern.

It is not approved for human use, carries no established human safety profile for physique use, and growth factors of this class are prohibited in sport. The exit from the body is far better understood than the long-term consequences of putting it in.

myths.myth: It's just a stronger, cleaner version of the IGF-1 your body already makes.
myths.truth: The very edit that makes it "stronger" — evading the binding proteins — is what makes it dangerous. Those binding proteins are a built-in safety brake, buffering how much active IGF-1 reaches your tissues. Cutting the leash removes the regulation, it doesn't purify the molecule.

myths.myth: It builds brand-new muscle fibres (hyperplasia) in humans, unlike steroids.
myths.truth: Satellite-cell activation and hyperplasia are shown mainly in cell and animal models. Solid human evidence that injecting IGF-1 LR3 adds new muscle fibres is essentially absent — the mechanism is real, but the human translation is unproven.

myths.myth: You can inject it locally to grow just one muscle ("site enhancement").
myths.truth: It enters the systemic circulation and signals body-wide through a receptor found on nearly every tissue. Reliable muscle-selective, local-only growth in humans is not established — the same signal is reaching your organs and any cancer-prone tissue.

myths.myth: It's legal because it's sold as a research chemical.
myths.truth: "Research chemical" describes how it's marketed to dodge regulation, not its safety or legality for you. No regulator has approved it for human use, it isn't a supplement, and it's banned in tested sport.

whenToUse.intro: Why people use it — and what it costs:
items: **Why people reach for it:** the pitch is a GH-level, or stronger, muscle-growth signal delivered directly — with the hope of true hyperplasia (new muscle cells) that steroids don't offer. The mechanism is genuinely potent; the human payoff for physique use is unproven.
**The blood-sugar cost:** its insulin-like action can cause hypoglycemia — sometimes severe. This is the most immediate, concrete danger, and it's why the watch-out list leads with it.

**The cancer and organ cost:** IGF1R is a pro-growth, anti-apoptosis switch on nearly every cell. Higher circulating IGF-1 is associated with increased risk of several cancers, and organ enlargement (including the heart) is a documented concern with sustained IGF-1 elevation.

**The evidence cost:** there are no human trials of injected IGF-1 LR3 for muscle building and no long-term human safety data. It's manufactured as a lab reagent, not a medicine. You would be the experiment.
**The product-quality cost:** gray-market peptides are routinely mislabeled, underdosed, overdosed or contaminated — so you often don't actually know the identity or amount of what you're injecting.
**The legal and testing cost:** not approved for human use, and prohibited in tested sport. This page exists to explain the pharmacology, not to help you run a protocol.

start.q: In one line: why inject IGF-1 LR3 instead of growth hormone?
start.a: Because GH barely acts on muscle directly — it works by making IGF-1, which is the molecule that actually drives growth. LR3 delivers that signal directly and, by evading the binding proteins, keeps it active far longer.

how.q: What did the "LR3" modifications change — and what did they deliberately leave alone?
how.a: They added a 13-amino-acid N-terminal extension and an Arg-for-Glu swap at position 3, dropping affinity for the IGF-binding proteins ~1,000-fold. They left IGF1R binding essentially intact — so the receptor signal is unchanged but far less regulated.

use.q: Why is hypoglycemia the risk that shows up first?
use.a: IGF-1 is an insulin cousin and binds the insulin receptor at low affinity, driving glucose into cells and suppressing the liver's glucose output. Push the dose and blood sugar can crash — fast and dangerously.

evidence.q: How strong is the human evidence for building muscle with it?
evidence.a: There essentially isn't any. IGF-1 LR3 is sold as a cell-culture reagent; there are no human muscle-building trials and no long-term safety data. The only approved IGF-1 (native mecasermin) is for severe deficiency, not physique.

canExplain: Why GH's muscle effect actually runs through IGF-1 — and why LR3 skips GH entirely
What the "LR3" edits change (binding-protein evasion) and, crucially, what they leave intact (receptor binding)
Why cutting the binding-protein leash removes a safety brake rather than making the molecule cleaner
Why it crashes blood sugar — the insulin-receptor connection
Why the same signal that grows muscle also raises organ-growth and cancer concerns, and why the human evidence is essentially absent

selfTest.q: A friend says IGF-1 LR3 is safer than steroids because "it's just natural IGF-1 that lasts longer." Give the two-part correction.
selfTest.a: (1) The reason it lasts longer is that it evades the IGF-binding proteins — and those proteins are a natural safety buffer, so "longer-lasting" means "less regulated," not "safer." (2) It still fires IGF1R on nearly every tissue, so it carries hypoglycemia, organ-growth and cancer-signal risks that have no human safety data behind them for this use.

selfTest.q: Why does IGF-1 LR3 cause hypoglycemia when it's marketed as a muscle drug, not a diabetes drug?
selfTest.a: IGF-1 shares a scaffold with insulin and binds the insulin receptor at low affinity. That's enough to pull glucose into cells and suppress hepatic glucose output, so blood sugar can fall dangerously — an unavoidable consequence of the molecule's insulin-like shape, not a dosing accident.

selfTest.q: The mechanism (satellite-cell activation, hyperplasia) sounds impressive. Why is that not enough to conclude it safely builds muscle in humans?
selfTest.a: Because most of that evidence is from cell and animal models. There are no human trials of injected IGF-1 LR3 for physique and no long-term safety data — a robust mechanism doesn't prove a real, safe human effect, and here the translation simply hasn't been tested.

evi.howStrong: For this use, the human evidence is essentially non-existent. IGF-1 LR3 is manufactured and sold as a laboratory cell-culture reagent (LONG R3 IGF-I), not as a medicine — its "purpose" in the catalog is to grow cells in a dish. There are no randomized human trials of injected IGF-1 LR3 for muscle building, and no long-term human safety data for that use. What IS well established is the underlying biology: IGF-1 signalling through IGF1R and the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway is a genuine, thoroughly characterized driver of muscle growth in cell and animal models. The gap is entirely in the translation — from a real mechanism to a proven, safe human physique effect.
evi.whatItShows: The mechanistic literature shows IGF-1 reliably activates muscle protein synthesis and satellite cells. It does NOT show that injecting IGF-1 LR3 safely and predictably builds muscle in healthy humans — that step has not been tested.
evi.effect: An honest number for human physique use cannot be given, because the studies don't exist. The only human IGF-1 dosing data come from native rhIGF-1 (mecasermin) used to treat severe IGF-1 deficiency, where the goal and population are entirely different — and where hypoglycemia and tissue overgrowth are recognized adverse effects even under medical supervision.
evi.whoBenefits: In legitimate medicine, only patients with severe primary IGF-1 deficiency benefit from IGF-1 (as mecasermin), for growth failure. Use by healthy people to build muscle is entirely off-label, gray-market and unstudied for safety.
evi.caveats: Treat every physique claim as extrapolation from mechanism, not from human outcome data. Layer on the concrete hazards — hypoglycemia, organ enlargement, an IGF-1/cancer-risk association in epidemiology — plus the fact that gray-market vials are frequently mislabeled or contaminated. A strong mechanism with no human safety trials is a reason for caution, not confidence.

tech.chem: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 IGF-1) is an 83-amino-acid recombinant analog of human insulin-like growth factor-1 (native IGF-1 is 70 residues). It carries two engineered changes: a 13-amino-acid N-terminal extension and an arginine substituted for glutamate at position 3. Together these lower affinity for the IGF-binding proteins (IGFBP-1 through -6) by roughly 1,000-fold while preserving the IGF1R-binding domain, yielding a molecule that circulates largely unbound and stays biologically active far longer than native IGF-1. It is a peptide (parenterally administered) and is commercially produced as a cell-culture supplement (LONG R3 IGF-I).

targets.action: agonist (full activation of the receptor tyrosine kinase)
targets.affinity: high (nanomolar-range), comparable to native IGF-1 — the LR3 edits were designed to preserve receptor binding
targets.role: the primary target; a receptor tyrosine kinase driving PI3K/Akt/mTOR (protein synthesis, survival) and Ras/MAPK (proliferation)

targets.action: low-affinity agonist
targets.affinity: low relative to IGF1R, but sufficient to produce insulin-like glucose lowering
targets.role: the basis of the hypoglycemia risk; IGF-1's structural kinship with insulin lets it cross-activate INSR (and hybrid IGF1R/INSR receptors)

tech.signaling: Ligand binding autophosphorylates the IGF1R tyrosine kinase, recruiting IRS-1/2 and Shc. Two arms follow: PI3K → Akt → mTOR/p70S6K drives muscle protein synthesis and suppresses atrophy genes (FoxO-controlled), while Akt also blocks apoptosis; and Ras → Raf → MEK → ERK/MAPK drives proliferation and differentiation. In skeletal muscle, IGF1R signalling additionally activates satellite (stem) cells, the route to added myonuclei and potential hyperplasia. The anti-apoptotic, pro-proliferative output of this same cascade in non-muscle tissue is what underlies the organ-growth and cancer-promotion concerns.

adme.absorb: Not orally bioavailable (a peptide); administered by injection, typically subcutaneous.
adme.distribute: Circulates largely free rather than IGFBP-bound, distributing to IGF1R-bearing tissues throughout the body — muscle, but also organs and the CNS periphery.
adme.metabolise: Cleared by proteolytic degradation and renal handling as for other peptide growth factors; the LR3 modifications slow degradation and reduce the IGFBP-mediated clearance that shortens native IGF-1's active life.
adme.excrete: Peptide fragments cleared renally. A frequently cited ~20–30 h half-life circulates in gray-market and preclinical sources but lacks confirmation from rigorous human pharmacokinetic studies.

tech.pgx: No validated compound-specific pharmacogenetic marker. Response and risk are modulated by baseline IGF-1/IGFBP status, IGF1R and insulin-receptor expression, and insulin sensitivity — all variable — so individual response, and especially hypoglycemia susceptibility, should be treated as unpredictable.
tech.dose: No approved human dose exists for physique use. Gray-market and anecdotal use commonly cites microgram-range daily subcutaneous doses, but these are unvalidated for safety or efficacy and are described here only for accuracy, not as guidance. The one legitimate human IGF-1 context is native rhIGF-1 (mecasermin) prescribed and monitored for severe primary IGF-1 deficiency — a different molecule, indication and setting.
tech.tolerance: Not a caffeine-style receptor tolerance. The dominant time-dependent consequences are cumulative: sustained IGF1R activation can drive organ enlargement and theoretically promote existing neoplasia, while the insulin-like activity poses a per-dose hypoglycemia hazard. Chronic ligand exposure can also downregulate receptor availability, but the governing concern is accumulating biological risk, not fading effect.

contrasts.title: IGF-1 LR3 vs native IGF-1 — the cut leash
contrasts.point: Same receptor, same growth signal — the difference is regulation. Your natural IGF-1 is held on a leash: binding proteins sequester ~99% of it and release only a controlled trickle, buffering how much reaches your tissues. IGF-1 LR3 is that molecule with the leash cut — the ~1,000-fold drop in binding-protein affinity is the whole point. So the "upgrade" over natural IGF-1 is precisely the removal of a built-in safety brake, which is why it acts longer, stronger and far less controllably.

contrasts.title: IGF-1 vs insulin — cousins that share a receptor
contrasts.point: IGF-1 and insulin are built on the same molecular scaffold and their receptors are close relatives that even form hybrids. That kinship is why IGF-1 LR3 spills onto the insulin receptor and can crash your blood sugar — the hypoglycemia isn't a contaminant or an accident, it's the predictable cost of a growth factor shaped like insulin.
